title: SEGMENT 6 — Reverse transcription, step by step

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — Reverse transcription, step by step

This is going to be the longest segment in this lecture, and I want you to bear with me, because if you understand reverse transcription you understand retroviruses, and if you don't understand reverse transcription you are going to spend the rest of your career taking certain things on faith.
So let's slow down.
The substrate for reverse transcription is a single-stranded positive-sense RNA.
The product is double-stranded DNA.
RT is the enzyme.

But the path from substrate to product is not a single uniform copying step — it involves two strand transfers, also called template switches, and the geometry of those transfers is what generates the long terminal repeats that you've already met as a key feature of the integrated provirus.
The whole architecture of the LTR exists because of how reverse transcription unfolds.
Let me walk you through it.
Start with the genomic RNA.
Five-prime to three-prime, it reads: R-U5-PBS-gag-pol-env-PPT-U3-R.
Notice the R sequence appears at both ends, the U5 at one end, the U3 at the other.
Step 1 — Primer binding.
Inside the virion, a host tRNA — tRNA-Lys-3 in HIV-1 — is already base-paired to the PBS.
So our starting configuration has a tRNA hanging off the genome, with its 3' end positioned right at the PBS, just downstream of U5.
RT recognizes this primer-template junction and begins to synthesize DNA, extending the 3' end of the tRNA.
Step 2 — Minus-strand strong-stop DNA.
RT walks backward — that is, in the 3'-to-5' direction along the RNA template, which means it synthesizes DNA in the 5'-to-3' direction — copying U5 and then R.
When it gets to the very 5' end of the genome, it stops.
There's no more template.
The DNA it has just made is a short complementary strand running from the PBS through U5 through R.
About 180 nucleotides for HIV-1.
This piece is called minus-strand strong-stop DNA.
Step 3 — RNase H degrades the template.
RT has a built-in second activity, RNase H, a domain that degrades RNA when it's hybridized to DNA.
As RT walks along the RNA template laying down DNA, RNase H follows behind chewing up the RNA.
So after the strong-stop DNA is made, the RNA U5 and R sequences at the 5' end of the genome have been digested.
What remains is a free 3' end on our new DNA, with R sequence ready to base-pair to something.
Step 4 — First strand transfer.
And here's where the magic starts.
Remember, R appears at both ends of the genome.
The R sequence on the new DNA strand is complementary to the R sequence at the 3' end of the genome — the other end.

So the freshly synthesized minus-strand strong-stop DNA, with its dangling 3' R sequence, hops over to the 3' end of the same RNA molecule — or, often, to the 3' end of the other RNA molecule in the diploid virion — and base-pairs to R there.
This is the first strand transfer.
If you can hold this picture in your head, it's the key to the whole process.
The DNA primer made from the 5' end of the genome is now repositioned at the 3' end, base-paired to R, with its 3' OH pointing inward toward the rest of the template.
Step 5 — Elongation of minus-strand DNA.
RT now extends from this transferred primer, copying U3, then env, pol, gag, PBS, all the way back toward the original 5' end of the template.
RNase H follows behind, chewing up the RNA template.
The product is now a full-length minus-strand DNA — that is, a DNA complementary to almost the entire genome.
There is one stretch of RNA that RNase H does not digest efficiently, and that's the polypurine tract, the PPT.
The PPT is resistant to RNase H cleavage and remains base-paired to the new DNA after the rest of the template is gone.
That resistant RNA fragment becomes the primer for plus-strand DNA synthesis.
Step 6 — Plus-strand strong-stop DNA.
RT now uses that PPT RNA fragment as a primer and copies the minus-strand DNA template.
It synthesizes through U3, R, U5, and into the PBS region.
Then it stops, because the tRNA at the original PBS, which has now been copied as DNA, contains a modified base — typically methyl-A — that RT cannot read through.
This second strong-stop product is called plus-strand strong-stop DNA.
It contains U3, R, U5, and the first 18 nucleotides of the PBS.
Step 7 — Second strand transfer.
Once again, RNase H removes the tRNA primer that started the whole reaction.
Now we have a plus-strand DNA segment ending in PBS sequence, and a minus-strand DNA ending in PBS sequence at the other end.
They can base-pair to each other through PBS.
The plus-strand DNA hops over and anneals to the 3' end of the minus-strand DNA.
This is the second strand transfer.
Step 8 — Bidirectional elongation.
From this configuration, RT extends in both directions, completing both strands.
The plus strand fills in the rest of the genome on the minus-strand template, and the minus strand fills in to the end of the plus-strand template.
The final product is linear double-stranded DNA with full-length LTRs at both ends.
Look at what just happened.
The original RNA had U5 at the 5' end and U3 at the 3' end, with R at both ends.
The DNA product has both U3 and U5 at both ends.
The first LTR — U3-R-U5 — was assembled by combining U3 from the 3' end of the template with R and U5 from the 5' end of the template.
The two strand transfers, the two strong-stop products, exist precisely to construct those LTRs.
The LTRs are not pre-existing structures in the RNA.
They are generated during reverse transcription by stitching the ends of the genome together.
That is reverse transcription.
RNA in.
Linear dsDNA out, with brand-new LTRs at both ends.
It takes a few hours in a cell.
It produces, on average, about one mutation per genome, because RT has no proofreading exonuclease.
That error rate is going to become enormously important when we talk about drug resistance.
The next step is to get this DNA into a chromosome.
